title: The Man Versus the State
description: The Man Versus the State — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "个体与国家" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: Adam Smith
memes.description: Scottish economist and moral philosopher, author of The Wealth of Nations (1776) and The Theory of Moral Sentiments (1759); his theory of sympathy was adopted by Herbert Spencer to explain the origin of the moral sense.

memes.label: Adaptation
memes.description: The process by which an organism or society modifies its constitution to achieve harmony with its conditions — the fundamental mechanism by which Spencer explained the diminution of evil and the possibility of moral progress.

memes.label: Administration of Justice
memes.description: The state's function of adjudicating disputes and enforcing rights — which Spencer identified as the primary legitimate duty of government, while exposing the corruptions and inefficiencies of its actual administration in nineteenth-century England.

memes.label: Isaac Newton
memes.description: English mathematician, physicist, and astronomer whose laws of motion and universal gravitation established the paradigm of modern physical science. Herbert Spencer invoked Newton as the exemplar of systematic scientific method against the empirical guesswork of legislative politics.

memes.label: Afghan War
memes.description: "The First Anglo-Afghan War (1839–1842), a British imperial military expedition that cost £17,000,000 — cited by Herbert Spencer as a prime example of the financial and moral costs of aggressive colonialism pursued under the pretext of expanding commerce."

memes.label: Aggregation
memes.description: The process by which separate units — individuals, families, tribes — coalesce into larger, more complex social wholes; for Spencer, aggregation was the first stage of social evolution, the foundational movement from dispersion to concentration that makes all subsequent differentiation and integration possible.

memes.label: Alexis de Tocqueville
memes.description: French political thinker and historian whose analysis of the Ancien Régime and the French Revolution was cited by Herbert Spencer to demonstrate how excessive state regulation leads to societal collapse.

memes.label: Aristocracy
memes.description: A form of government in which political power is held by a privileged minority class — analysed by Herbert Spencer as a transitional stage in the historical movement from despotism toward democracy and, ultimately, complete individual liberty.

memes.label: Auguste Comte
memes.description: French philosopher and founder of positivism and sociology, who divided social philosophy into statics and dynamics — a framework that Herbert Spencer adopted and acknowledged in his own social theory.
aliases: Isidore Auguste Marie François Xavier Comte

memes.label: Beneficence
memes.description: In Herbert Spencer's ethical system, beneficence is the sphere of moral action beyond justice — divided into negative beneficence (refraining from harm) and positive beneficence (actively doing good), with the latter being supererogatory rather than coercively enforceable.

memes.label: Black Act 1723
memes.description: The Black Act of 1723 (9 Geo. I c. 22), a notoriously severe English statute that created over 50 capital offences for poaching and related activities, which Herbert Spencer cited as a paradigmatic example of class-based legislation where state power was wielded to serve the interests of the propertied few at the expense of the many.

memes.label: British Parliament
memes.description: The supreme legislative body of the United Kingdom, consisting of the House of Commons and House of Lords. Central subject of Herbert Spencer's critique of majoritarian democracy and legislative overreach.

memes.label: C. Forrest
memes.description: East London builder and contractor of forty years' experience, personally consulted by Herbert Spencer as an expert witness on how building regulations and taxation inflated the cost of working-class housing.

memes.label: Catholic Emancipation
memes.description: The removal of most civil and political disabilities imposed on Roman Catholics in the United Kingdom, enacted by the Roman Catholic Relief Act 1829. A landmark in the extension of religious liberty, cited by Herbert Spencer as an instance of the state's proper withdrawal from religious coercion.

memes.label: Charles Darwin
memes.description: English naturalist whose theory of evolution by natural selection, published in On the Origin of Species (1859), transformed biology and deeply influenced Herbert Spencer's social and political philosophy.

memes.label: Charles II
memes.description: King of England, Scotland, and Ireland (1660–1685) whose reign, colonial policies, and sumptuary legislation were cited by Herbert Spencer as examples of monarchical overreach, the arrogation of unchecked power, and the arbitrary regulation of private life.

memes.label: Charles Lyell
memes.description: Scottish geologist and the leading exponent of uniformitarianism, whose career illustrated what Herbert Spencer called the tendency to retain old theological beliefs under new scientific forms — an analogy Spencer applied to the "great political superstition" of parliamentary omnipotence.

memes.label: Chartism
memes.description: "The Chartist movement (1838–1857), a mass working-class campaign for political reform in Britain centred on the 'People's Charter' of six demands — cited by Herbert Spencer as an expression of the same 'kindred spirit' of moral evolution that drove all progressive political movements, from Catholic Emancipation to the Reform Bills."

memes.label: Church of England
memes.description: The officially established Christian church in England, analyzed by Herbert Spencer as an instance of state involvement in religion — a domain he argued should be left entirely to voluntary, non-coercive association.

memes.label: Common Law
memes.description: The body of law derived from custom and judicial precedent rather than legislative enactment — which Spencer invoked as evidence that rights precede and constrain government rather than being created by it.

memes.label: Communism
memes.description: An economic and social doctrine proposing collective or state ownership of land and the means of production — critiqued by Herbert Spencer as ethically incompatible with the law of equal freedom and practically unworkable in a society that respects individual rights.

memes.label: Compulsory Cooperation
memes.description: In Spencer's social theory, the form of social coordination in which individuals' contributions to collective life are assigned by authority rather than chosen by voluntary agreement — the defining characteristic of the militant type of society.

memes.label: Contagious Diseases Act
memes.description: Series of British statutes (1864, 1866, 1869) that gave police powers to detain and forcibly examine women suspected of prostitution in garrison towns — condemned by Spencer as abolishing fundamental safeguards to individual liberty under the guise of public health.

memes.label: Corn Laws
memes.description: British tariffs and restrictions on grain imports (1815–1846), repealed after decades of agitation — cited by Spencer as a paradigm case of class legislation and an object lesson in how the state enriches the few at the expense of the many.

memes.label: David Brewster
memes.description: Scottish physicist and science advocate whose proposal for a State-funded "intellectual priesthood" of scientists was critiqued by Herbert Spencer as exemplifying the overreach of government intervention.

memes.label: Declaration of Independence
memes.description: The 1776 declaration by the Thirteen Colonies asserting independence from Great Britain, which Herbert Spencer cited both as a landmark affirmation of natural equality of human rights and as a contemporary indictment of mother-country colonial misgovernment.

memes.label: Democracy
memes.description: A system of government based on popular representation and majority decision-making — which Spencer regarded as a necessary check on state power but warned against as a potential instrument of class legislation and the tyranny of the majority.

memes.label: Despotism
memes.description: The concentration of arbitrary power in a ruler or ruling body — which Spencer argued persists when absolute monarchy is replaced by legislative majority rather than by genuine limitation of government authority.

memes.label: Dissolution
memes.description: The process by which integrated social structures break down into their constituent parts — in Spencer's evolutionary framework, dissolution is the inverse of evolution, the complementary process through which homogeneity is restored when the conditions sustaining integration are removed.

memes.label: Divine Right
memes.description: The doctrine that a ruler or ruling body derives authority directly from divine delegation — analyzed and rejected by Herbert Spencer as a political superstition that has merely shifted its object from monarchs to parliaments and majorities.

memes.label: Divine Right of Kings
memes.description: The doctrine that monarchs derive their authority directly from God and are accountable to no earthly power — identified by Spencer as "the great political superstition of the past," superseded by the equally superstitious "divine right of parliaments."

memes.label: Division of Labour
memes.description: The process by which social functions become progressively specialized and differentiated — identified by Spencer as the fundamental mechanism of social evolution, driving the transition from simple homogeneous societies to complex heterogeneous ones.

memes.label: Edmund Burke
memes.description: Anglo-Irish statesman and political philosopher, author of Reflections on the Revolution in France (1790), foundational figure of modern conservatism; Herbert Spencer references Burke on sympathy in the context of the moral sentiments.

memes.label: Emperor Julian (the Apostate)
memes.description: 罗马皇帝（361–363），以恢复异教信仰闻名，其限制教育自由的政策被 Spencer 引为国家压制知识传播的历史先例。

memes.label: Erskine May
memes.description: English constitutional historian and clerk of the House of Commons, author of Constitutional History of England, cited by Herbert Spencer on the human cost of the Corn Laws.

memes.label: Expediency
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's term for the method of political decision-making that judges each proposed state action by its anticipated utility in isolation, without reference to any general limiting principle — a method he argues is inherently arbitrary, self-defeating, and the intellectual engine of expanding state power.

memes.label: Factory Acts
memes.description: A series of 19th-century British laws that progressively extended state regulation of working conditions in factories, which Herbert Spencer analyzed as a central example of the "New Toryism" — the reversal of classical liberal principles through compulsory legislation.

memes.label: Feudalism
memes.description: A social and political system characterized by hierarchical land tenure, personal allegiance, and military obligation — for Spencer, feudalism represented a specific phase in social evolution between primitive tribalism and modern industrial society, and its lingering mental habits constituted a persistent obstacle to genuine liberalism.

memes.label: Francis Bacon
memes.description: English philosopher, statesman, and Lord Chancellor whose advocacy of empirical induction laid the foundation for modern scientific method. Herbert Spencer cited Bacon as an example of how great intellect does not guarantee personal integrity.

memes.label: François Guizot
memes.description: French historian and statesman, Prime Minister under Louis-Philippe, whose observation that belief in the "sovereign power of political machinery" is "a gross delusion" was extensively quoted by Herbert Spencer in his critique of government overreach in Social Statics.

memes.label: Frank Fairman
memes.description: British socialist writer who, under the pseudonym "Frank Fairman," published a critical reply to Herbert Spencer's anti-socialist arguments in The Man Versus the State.

memes.label: Free Trade
memes.description: The principle that commercial exchange should be unhindered by state-imposed tariffs, restrictions, or regulations — defended by Spencer as a corollary of the law of equal freedom and a defining feature of industrial society.

memes.label: French Revolution
memes.description: The 1789–1799 revolutionary upheaval in France, which Herbert Spencer analyzed as both a catastrophic consequence of excessive state regulation and a cautionary tale about the misuse of classical republican models.

memes.label: George Grote
memes.description: English historian of ancient Greece, cited by Herbert Spencer as an authority on the absence of hero-worship in classical Greek political culture.

memes.label: Granville Sharp
memes.description: English abolitionist and philanthropist whose assertion of natural human equality was cited by Herbert Spencer alongside Locke, Filmer, and Blackstone as evidence of the universal intuition of equal liberty.

memes.label: Greatest Happiness
memes.description: The utilitarian ethical maxim — "the greatest happiness of the greatest number" — critically examined by Herbert Spencer in Social Statics as an immediate aim that cannot be pursued directly but must be sought indirectly through the law of equal freedom.

memes.label: Habeas Corpus Act 1679
memes.description: Landmark English statute that strengthened the writ of habeas corpus, preventing arbitrary imprisonment by requiring that detained persons be brought before a court — cited by Spencer as the archetypal Whig measure protecting individual liberty against state coercion.

memes.label: Francis Bacon
memes.description: English philosopher, statesman, and Lord Chancellor whose advocacy of empirical induction laid the foundation for modern scientific method. Herbert Spencer cited Bacon as an example of how great intellect does not guarantee personal integrity.

memes.label: Henry Hyndman
memes.description: English socialist leader and founder of the Social Democratic Federation; author of "Socialism and Slavery," a critique of Herbert Spencer's anti-socialist essay "The Coming Slavery."

memes.label: Henry Rowe Schoolcraft
memes.description: American geographer, geologist, and ethnologist whose studies of Native American societies, particularly the Ojibwe (Chippewayans), provided Herbert Spencer with anthropological evidence that property rights predate formal government.

memes.label: Henry St John, Viscount Bolingbroke
memes.description: English politician and political philosopher whose analysis of the Whig–Tory distinction was quoted extensively by Herbert Spencer in his critique of the "New Toryism."

memes.label: Herbert Spencer
memes.description: English philosopher, biologist, and political theorist (1820–1903). Author of Social Statics and The Man Versus the State, originator of the "law of equal freedom" and a foundational figure in classical liberalism and sociology.

memes.label: Hippolyte Taine
memes.description: French historian and critic whose analysis of the Ancien Régime provided Herbert Spencer with empirical evidence of the destructive effects of excessive state regulation.

memes.label: House of Lords
memes.description: The upper house of the Parliament of the United Kingdom, historically composed of hereditary peers and bishops of the Church of England. Herbert Spencer, writing in the mid-to-late nineteenth century, critiqued the hereditary principle in legislation as incompatible with the equal-freedom principle.

memes.label: Individualism
memes.description: The doctrine, central to Herbert Spencer's political philosophy, that the individual is the fundamental unit of moral and political concern — that each person's faculties must be free to develop, bounded only by the equal freedom of all others, and that collective action must be justified in terms of its effects on individuals.

memes.label: Industrial Type
memes.description: In Spencer's social typology, the form of social organization characterized by voluntary cooperation, the regime of contract, and limited governmental authority — contrasted with the militant type.

memes.label: Industrialism
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's term for the type of social organization characterized by voluntary cooperation, the regime of contract, and limited government — historically associated with peaceful commercial societies and opposed to the militant type based on compulsory cooperation and status.

memes.label: Louis Blanc
memes.description: French socialist politician and theorist whose advocacy of state-controlled production was critiqued by Herbert Spencer in Social Statics as exemplifying the overreach of governmental power.

memes.label: Jeremy Bentham
memes.description: English philosopher, jurist, and social reformer (1748–1832). Founder of modern utilitarianism, articulating the principle of "the greatest happiness of the greatest number" as the foundation of morals and legislation.

memes.label: John Austin
memes.description: 英国法律实证主义创始人，其主权命令理论被 Spencer 在《人对国家》中作为国家绝对权威论的典型加以批判。

memes.label: John Elliott Cairnes
memes.description: Irish political economist and Professor at University College London; critic of Herbert Spencer's social evolution doctrine, arguing that Spencer underestimated the role of higher motives in social progress.

memes.label: John Locke
memes.description: English philosopher and physician (1632–1704), widely regarded as the father of classical liberalism. His theory of natural rights — life, liberty, and property — and government by consent profoundly influenced Herbert Spencer's political philosophy.

memes.label: John Stuart Mill
memes.description: English philosopher, political economist, and Member of Parliament (1806–1873). The most influential nineteenth-century British liberal theorist; author of *On Liberty* (1859), the canonical defense of individual freedom against social and governmental coercion.

memes.label: Joseph Chamberlain
memes.description: 英国政治家，伯明翰激进改革的推动者，先后为自由党和自由统一党议员，以市政社会主义和地方税改革著称。

memes.label: Justice
memes.description: The primary ethical limit on individual conduct — the enforcement of equal freedom, forbidding aggression and breach of contract. In Herbert Spencer's system, justice is the measurable, scientific half of morality, distinguished from beneficence.

memes.label: Kaffir Wars
memes.description: A series of 19th-century conflicts between British colonists and the Xhosa people on the Cape Colony's eastern frontier, which Herbert Spencer cited as a consequence of the inherently aggressive character of state-managed colonization, contrasting it unfavourably with the peaceable private settlement of Pennsylvania under William Penn.

memes.label: Laissez-Faire
memes.description: The doctrine that government should minimize interference in economic and social affairs, limiting itself to the protection of person and property — a principle Spencer defended as the structural requirement of an industrial society.

memes.label: Law of Equal Freedom
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's foundational moral principle that every man is free to exercise his faculties so long as he does not trespass upon the like freedom of any other — the law of right social relationships derived from the conditions of greatest happiness.

memes.label: Law of Nature
memes.description: The principle, recognized across legal traditions from Roman civil law to English common law, that certain rules of justice exist independently of legislative enactment — invoked by Herbert Spencer to argue that rights are discovered by law, not created by it.

memes.label: Liberalism
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's conception of true liberalism as the doctrine of individual freedom versus State coercion — historically grounded in the removal of restraints, and fundamentally opposed to the "new Toryism" of coercive welfare legislation.

memes.label: Liberty
memes.description: The freedom of each individual to exercise their faculties, limited only by the like freedom of all others — Herbert Spencer's first principle of ethics and politics, grounding all specific rights.

memes.label: Licensing Acts
memes.description: A series of 19th-century British laws regulating the sale of alcohol through compulsory licensing, which Herbert Spencer critiqued as a form of state paternalism that eroded individual responsibility and created organized political interests.

memes.label: Lord Lyttelton
memes.description: British peer and Liberal politician who chaired a memorial to Gladstone cataloguing the failures of state-managed public works, cited by Herbert Spencer as evidence of governmental incompetence.

memes.label: Lord Palmerston
memes.description: British statesman, twice Prime Minister (1855–1858, 1859–1865); a dominant figure of mid-Victorian politics whose legislative interventions — including factory acts and public health acts — were cited by Herbert Spencer as examples of excessive state interference.

memes.label: Louis Blanc
memes.description: French socialist politician and theorist whose advocacy of state-controlled production was critiqued by Herbert Spencer in Social Statics as exemplifying the overreach of governmental power.

memes.label: Magna Carta
memes.description: The "Great Charter" sealed by King John at Runnymede in 1215, establishing the principle that the monarch is subject to the law. A foundational document in the Anglo-American constitutional tradition, referenced by Herbert Spencer in his analysis of the evolution of limited government.

memes.label: Martin Luther
memes.description: 德国宗教改革家，新教路德宗的创立者，其对待农民起义的态度被 Spencer 用作"道德感由情感而非理性决定"的例证。

memes.label: Matthew Arnold
memes.description: English poet and cultural critic, author of Culture and Anarchy; cited by Herbert Spencer as an example of the denial of natural rights, particularly regarding property and copyright.

memes.label: Mechanism
memes.description: The institutional apparatus and procedural machinery through which the state exercises its functions — which Spencer argued inevitably grows beyond its proper scope, producing officialism, inefficiency, and the erosion of individual liberty.

memes.label: Merchant Shipping Acts
memes.description: A series of 19th-century British laws regulating merchant vessels, seamen's conditions, and maritime safety, which Herbert Spencer analyzed as an example of how state intervention generates unintended consequences and special-interest capture.

memes.label: Militancy
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's term for the type of social organization characterized by compulsory cooperation, the regime of status, and centralized authority — historically associated with warlike societies and opposed to the industrial type based on voluntary cooperation and contract.

memes.label: Militant Type
memes.description: In Spencer's social typology, the form of social organization characterized by compulsory cooperation, the regime of status, and centralized governmental control — contrasted with the industrial type.

memes.label: Monarchy
memes.description: A system of government in which supreme authority is vested in a single hereditary ruler — which Spencer analyzed as the political expression of the militant type of society, historically necessary but progressively displaced by industrial development.

memes.label: Moral Sense
memes.description: The innate human faculty, identified by Shaftesbury, Hutcheson, and defended by Herbert Spencer, through which individuals intuitively perceive moral right and wrong — a faculty refined by evolution and serving as one route to the law of equal freedom.

memes.label: Moral Sense Doctrine
memes.description: The theory, defended by Spencer against utilitarian critics, that human beings possess an innate faculty — the "moral sense" — which prompts upright conduct, analogous to the instinctual desires that secure bodily welfare.

memes.label: Mr. Arch
memes.description: 19 世纪英国激进协会人物，主张通过立法强制耕种土地以提高农业产出，被 Spencer 引为土地国有化趋势的案例。

memes.label: Napoleon Bonaparte
memes.description: French military leader and emperor (1769–1821) whose imperial ambition and moral character were cited by Spencer to illustrate the limits of intellect in producing virtue.

memes.label: Natural Rights
memes.description: The doctrine that individuals possess certain rights by virtue of their humanity, not by grant of the state. Herbert Spencer defended natural rights on evolutionary grounds, distinguishing his position from both Benthamite positivism and Lockean theism.

memes.label: Natural Selection
memes.description: The Darwinian mechanism of evolutionary change through differential survival — adopted by Herbert Spencer as both a biological fact and a social principle, who argued that state interventions which suspend the "never-ceasing discipline of experience" retard social adaptation and promote "survival of the unfittest."

memes.label: Negative Beneficence
memes.description: In Herbert Spencer's ethical taxonomy, the secondary limitation on conduct — the duty to refrain from acts that, while not directly violating equal freedom, would cause unhappiness to others; distinguished from justice, which is the primary limitation forbidding direct infringement of equal freedom.

memes.label: New Buildings Act
memes.description: The Metropolitan Buildings Act and subsequent building regulations that, according to Spencer, worsened the housing conditions of London's poor by suppressing private construction and increasing overcrowding — a central case study in his argument against legislative intervention.

memes.label: Oligarchy
memes.description: A form of government in which power is concentrated in the hands of a small group — conceived by Spencer as an intermediate stage in political evolution between despotism and democracy, and a persistent structural feature of militant societies in which the few exercise unchecked authority over the many.

memes.label: Otto von Bismarck
memes.description: 普鲁士首相、德意志帝国首任宰相，其国家社会主义倾向被 Spencer 在《即将到来的奴役》中作为国家权力扩张的典型警告。

memes.label: Pierre-Joseph Proudhon
memes.description: French philosopher and socialist, widely regarded as the father of anarchism, known for his declaration that "property is robbery" — a thesis that Herbert Spencer critically examined in his defence of private property rights in Social Statics.

memes.label: Political Economy
memes.description: The study of the production, distribution, and consumption of wealth as governed by natural laws, which Herbert Spencer regarded as an essential branch of social science revealing the unintended consequences of state intervention.

memes.label: Political Ethics
memes.description: The moral principles governing the conduct of governments and legislators toward citizens — a subject Spencer treated as historically derived from military ethics and only gradually diverging from the ethics of war as societies evolve toward the industrial type.

memes.label: Political Superstition
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's term for the unjustified belief that governmental authority — whether of kings, parliaments, or majorities — is unlimited in scope, a belief he traces from monarchical divine right through to modern democratic majoritarianism.

memes.label: Poor Law
memes.description: A system of state-administered relief for the destitute, which Herbert Spencer criticized as a harmful intervention that undermines natural adaptation and increases the misery it intends to alleviate.

memes.label: Poor Law Amendment Act 1834
memes.description: British legislation that radically reformed the system of poor relief by centralizing administration under the Poor Law Commission and introducing the workhouse system, shaped by utilitarian and Malthusian principles.

memes.label: Poor Law Commission
memes.description: The central administrative body established by the Poor Law Amendment Act 1834 to oversee the reformed system of poor relief in England and Wales. For Herbert Spencer, the Commission exemplified the Benthamite appetite for centralized bureaucratic control over local and voluntary action.

memes.label: Pope Alexander VI
memes.description: 罗马教皇（1492–1503），其划分西葡殖民势力范围的教皇诏书被 Spencer 引为国家殖民恶政的历史案例。

memes.label: Positive Law
memes.description: Law that derives its authority from the command of a sovereign, whether a monarch, aristocracy, or democratic majority — the conception of law advanced by John Austin and critiqued by Herbert Spencer as presupposing an unlimited sovereignty for which no warrant can be given.

memes.label: Principles of Ethics
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's major work on moral philosophy, in which he grounded ethics in the law of equal freedom and the evolutionary process of adaptation — treating morality as the progressive adjustment of human conduct to the requirements of social life.

memes.label: Property
memes.description: The right of exclusive possession and use of material and intellectual goods, derived by Herbert Spencer from the law of equal freedom and defended against both socialism and Benthamite positivism.

memes.label: Public Health
memes.description: The nineteenth-century movement for state-led sanitary and health improvement — analyzed and critiqued by Herbert Spencer in Social Statics and Man Versus the State as a case study in the expansion of governmental power beyond its legitimate protective function into paternalistic supervision of private life.

memes.label: Public Health Acts
memes.description: "A series of nineteenth-century British statutes establishing state sanitary supervision, compulsory health inspection, and local board powers — cited by Herbert Spencer as a key instance of the modern expansion of state coercion into new domains formerly left to individual and local action."

memes.label: Public Libraries Act 1850
memes.description: The Public Libraries Act of 1850, which authorized English municipal boroughs to establish free public libraries funded by local rates, which Herbert Spencer cited as an emblematic instance of the communistic drift of modern legislation — majority-imposed taxation compelling some citizens to pay for the books of others.

memes.label: Queen Elizabeth I
memes.description: Queen of England (1558–1603) whose colonial grants to Sir Humphrey Gilbert were cited by Herbert Spencer as an example of rulers presuming authority over lands and peoples they had never seen.

memes.label: Radicalism
memes.description: A political tendency advocating fundamental reform of existing institutions — analysed by Spencer as a manifestation of the same spirit of class antagonism that characterised the aristocratic and conservative forces it opposed, both parties sharing a uniform moral character shaped by the same social conditions.

memes.label: Ralph Waldo Emerson
memes.description: American essayist and transcendentalist philosopher, quoted by Herbert Spencer on the relation between abstract principles and concrete facts.

memes.label: Reform Act 1832
memes.description: British legislation that reformed the electoral system, expanding the franchise and redistributing parliamentary seats — cited by Spencer as an exemplar of Whig principles reducing coercive government power.

memes.label: Representative Government
memes.description: A system of government in which citizens delegate legislative and executive functions to elected representatives — examined by Herbert Spencer as a transitional form between monarchical despotism and a future regime of fully realized individual rights, structurally prone to legislative overreach.

memes.label: Richard Cobden
memes.description: English manufacturer and Radical statesman, leading figure of the Anti-Corn Law League, advocate of free trade and international peace; cited by Herbert Spencer in arguments against state regulation of labour.

memes.label: Right of Property
memes.description: The individual's moral claim to the exclusive use of that which his labour has produced or legitimately acquired — a natural right which Spencer argued is a direct corollary of the law of equal freedom and precedes all legislative recognition.

memes.label: Rights
memes.description: The moral claims of individuals to life, liberty, and property, derived by Herbert Spencer from the law of equal freedom and grounded in the constitution of human nature rather than in legislative enactment or social contract.

memes.label: Robert Filmer
memes.description: 英国 17 世纪政治哲学家，绝对君主制和君权神授论的代表性辩护者，被 Spencer 在《社会静力学》中列为否认人类自然权利的首要论敌。

memes.label: Rowland Hill
memes.description: English postal reformer who originated the penny post system; cited by Herbert Spencer as an example of private-enterprise innovation overcoming official resistance.

memes.label: Samuel Morley
memes.description: British philanthropist, Liberal MP, and education reformer who admired Herbert Spencer's Social Statics and privately reprinted Spencer's chapter on national education as a pamphlet.

memes.label: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
memes.description: English poet, literary critic, and philosopher of the Romantic movement, whose theories of life and the intellect were cited by Herbert Spencer in his analysis of individuation and moral development in Social Statics.

memes.label: Sanitary Supervision
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's term for state intervention in public health — encompassing medical licensing, housing regulation, and sanitation laws — which he argued violates individual liberty, retards social adaptation, and is less effective than voluntary action.

memes.label: Friedrich Wilhelm Joseph Schelling
memes.description: German idealist philosopher whose concept of a "tendency to individuation" was acknowledged by Herbert Spencer as a philosophical precursor to his own theory of universal evolution.

memes.label: Servile Wars
memes.description: "A series of armed uprisings by serfs and peasants against feudal lords during the Middle Ages — cited by Herbert Spencer to illustrate the moral symmetry between oppressor and oppressed under conditions of incomplete social evolution."
aliases: Servile Wars of the Middle Ages

memes.label: Abolition of the Slave Trade
memes.description: The British movement to end the transatlantic slave trade (culminating in the Slave Trade Act 1807) and slavery itself (Slavery Abolition Act 1833), examined by Herbert Spencer as an instance of unintended legislative consequences.

memes.label: Social Evolution
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's theory that human societies develop through a universal process of differentiation and integration, progressing from simple, homogeneous structures to complex, heterogeneous ones — applied to explain the transition from militant to industrial social types.

memes.label: Social Organism
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's analogy between human society and a biological organism, used to explain the division of labor, the interdependence of parts, and the self-regulating character of social systems that legislative interference disrupts.

memes.label: Social Statics
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's first major work (1851), which laid out the philosophical foundation for his entire synthetic philosophy — arguing that the conditions for greatest human happiness are fixed by the nature of man and society, not by legislative will.
aliases: The Conditions Essential to Human Happiness

memes.label: Socialism
memes.description: A system of social organization in which land and the means of production are held in common, with produce shared among members — examined and rejected by Herbert Spencer in Social Statics as incompatible with the law of equal freedom and the right of property.

memes.label: Sovereignty
memes.description: The supreme political authority within a territory, critically examined by Herbert Spencer as the "Great Political Superstition" — the unwarranted belief in unlimited majority rule.

memes.label: Specialization
memes.description: The process by which social functions become progressively differentiated into distinct, specialized roles — for Spencer, specialization was the central mechanism of social evolution, driving the transition from simple, homogeneous societies to complex, heterogeneous ones, and paralleling the differentiation of biological organs in higher organisms.

memes.label: Sphere of Government
memes.description: The proper scope and limits of governmental authority — a central question in Spencer's political philosophy, which held that the state's legitimate sphere is confined to the protection of individual rights and that any extension beyond this boundary constitutes an overstepping that leads to political superstition and social instability.

memes.label: Spitalfields Act 1773
memes.description: "British legislation of 1773 fixing minimum wages for Spitalfields silk-weavers, which led to the collapse of the local industry — cited by Herbert Spencer as a paradigmatic case of the unintended harms of legislative interference with the law of supply and demand."

memes.label: Stanley Jevons
memes.description: 英国经济学家，边际效用革命先驱。Spencer 在《社会静力学》和《人对国家》中与其就货币发行、国家干预和自然权利问题展开论辩。

memes.label: State Authority
memes.description: The proper scope and limits of governmental power in Herbert Spencer's political philosophy — confined to the protection of individual rights through the administration of justice, with every extension beyond this sphere constituting an illegitimate encroachment upon liberty.

memes.label: State Interference
memes.description: The action of the state in regulating or controlling activities that Spencer argued should remain within the sphere of individual liberty — a core theme of his critique of expanding government power in The Man Versus the State.

memes.label: State Socialism
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's term for the gradual transformation of liberal societies into systems of comprehensive state control — a process he argued was already underway in late-Victorian Britain through the accumulation of well-intentioned but individually modest legislative interventions, each extending the scope of compulsory cooperation.

memes.label: Survival of the Fittest
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's own term for the mechanism of natural selection — adopted by Darwin — which Spencer applied to social policy, arguing that state interventions that preserve the "unfittest" produce cumulative social deterioration across generations.

memes.label: The Coming Slavery
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's prophetic warning that the accumulation of well-intentioned state interventions would progressively restrict individual liberty, creating a new form of servitude administered by an expanding bureaucratic state.

memes.label: The Man Versus the State
memes.description: Herbert Spencer's 1884 critique of expanding state power — arguing that modern legislative interventionism constitutes a "New Toryism" that threatens individual freedom through compulsory regulation, reversing the historical liberal project of dismantling restraints.

memes.label: Thomas Carlyle
memes.description: Scottish historian, philosopher, and essayist, known for the "great man" theory of history and authoritarian solutions to social problems; Herbert Spencer critiqued his coercive proposals and diagnosed his residual Calvinism despite his rejection of formal religion.

memes.label: Thomas Chalmers
memes.description: 苏格兰牧师、政治经济学家和社会改革家。Spencer 在《社会静力学》中批判了其关于地主收入不影响社会总财富的经济学谬误。

memes.label: Thomas Farrer
memes.description: 英国统计学家、公务改革者和自由贸易倡导者，其著作《国家与贸易之关系》被 Spencer 在《人对国家》中反复引用来论证国家干预的系统性失败。

memes.label: Thomas Hobbes
memes.description: English philosopher, founder of modern political philosophy, known for his social contract theory and the justification of absolute sovereignty as presented in Leviathan (1651).

memes.label: Thomas Henry Huxley
memes.description: English biologist and anthropologist, known as "Darwin's Bulldog" for his advocacy of evolution by natural selection; engaged Herbert Spencer in debate on evolutionary ethics.

memes.label: Thomas Spencer
memes.description: English clergyman, incumbent of Hinton Charterhouse near Bath, known for his parish welfare work and as Herbert Spencer's uncle whose experience with the Poor Law informed Spencer's critique of state welfare.

memes.label: Toryism
memes.description: The political doctrine that government authority is inherently legitimate and expansible — which Spencer identified as the underlying ideology uniting both traditional Conservatives and the new breed of legislative interventionists in Victorian Britain.

memes.label: Tyranny
memes.description: The exercise of arbitrary power in violation of individual rights — which Spencer argued can manifest not only in political rule but in commercial regulation, domestic relations, and majority-imposed legislation.

memes.label: Utilitarianism
memes.description: The ethical and political doctrine, most influentially formulated by Jeremy Bentham, that the rightness of actions and policies is determined by their contribution to the greatest happiness — subjected by Herbert Spencer to sustained critique for its indefiniteness, its neglect of general principles, and its unintended service as intellectual justification for expanding state power.

memes.label: Walter Bagehot
memes.description: 英国经济学家、政论家，《经济学人》主编，在货币自由发行问题上与 Spencer 立场一致，反对国家垄断铸币权。

memes.label: William Cobbett
memes.description: 英国政治改革家与记者，主张"人人有权从土地中获得生计"，其济贫法主张和压制廉价读物的企图均遭到 Spencer 的批判。

memes.label: William Ewart Gladstone
memes.description: British Liberal statesman, four-time Prime Minister (1868–1894); Herbert Spencer criticized Gladstone's legislative program as exemplifying the expansion of state intervention that threatened individual liberty.

memes.label: William Penn
memes.description: English Quaker and founder of Pennsylvania; cited by Herbert Spencer as the exemplar of private, peaceful colonization in contrast to state-sponsored colonial violence.

memes.label: William Pitt (the Younger)
memes.description: 英国首相（1783–1801, 1804–1806），其济贫法政策被 Spencer 引为短视立法的典型案例——善意政策如何引发灾难性的长期后果。

memes.label: William Rathbone
memes.description: 英国自由党议员，关注地方税制和行政区域的混乱重叠问题，被 Spencer 引用以论证国家行政体系的系统性低效。

memes.label: Workshops Acts
memes.description: A series of 19th-century British laws extending factory-style regulation to smaller workshops and bakehouses, which Herbert Spencer analyzed as evidence of the relentless expansion of state control into ever more intimate domains of economic life.
